ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for buttery scone; not a clinical entity requiring SNOMED mapping.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard buttery scone weighs approximately 60 g, typical of a single tea-time serving.

nutrient_source.title: Buttery Scone — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Foodo entry for Buttery Scone (cr_id G00010283). Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for scones (USDA #18439) as HPB does not report fatty acid fractions separately; see notes.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Scone, plain
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 92 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain scone (white flour, baked). A buttery scone shares the same refined-wheat flour base and similar sugar content; the fat content from butter may marginally lower GI but the analogous plain scone value of 92 is used as the best available approved-source estimate. GL per serving calculated as (92 × 48.07 × 0.60) / 100 = 26.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 446 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g high-concern threshold; compounded by high sugar (13.4 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.2 g/100 g) driving insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit buttery scones due to the high sodium load (446 mg/100 g) and the additional blood-pressure burden from high refined-carbohydrate and sugar content; if consumed, a single small portion (≤30 g) without added jam, butter, or cream is advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~92) with 48.1 g carbohydrate/100 g and only 1.2 g dietary fibre/100 g, producing a glycemic load of ~26.6 per standard serving, causing rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Buttery scones are high-GI, high-GL foods that cause rapid postprandial glucose spikes and are not suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; if consumed occasionally, limit to a very small portion (≤30 g) and pair with a protein source such as plain Greek yogurt to attenuate the glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g from butter raises LDL cholesterol; combined with high sodium (446 mg/100 g), dietary cholesterol (74 mg/100 g), and refined carbohydrates, this food presents multiple cardiovascular risk factors.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit buttery scones due to high saturated fat (~10.5 g/100 g), high sodium (446 mg/100 g), and high refined-carbohydrate content, all of which adversely affect lipid profiles, blood pressure, and endothelial function.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat ~10.5 g/100 g from butter exceeds the RA pro-inflammatory threshold of 5 g/100 g; high sodium (446 mg/100 g) may worsen joint oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid buttery scones as the high saturated fat content (~10.5 g/100 g) promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity, and the high sodium content may exacerbate joint swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A buttery scone is a classic British baked good made from wheat flour, butter, milk, eggs, sugar, baking powder, and salt. The dough is mixed until just combined, shaped into rounds, and baked at high heat to produce a golden, crumbly exterior with a soft, layered interior. The generous butter content (responsible for approximately 10.5 g saturated fat per 100 g) gives the scone its characteristic rich, tender crumb and slightly flaky texture. Scones are a staple of British afternoon tea culture and are widely consumed in Singapore as a Western-style teatime treat, often served with clotted cream and jam. Nutritionally, a buttery scone is energy-dense at 382 kcal/100 g, with high carbohydrate content (48.1 g/100 g), high fat (18.4 g/100 g), moderate protein (6.7 g/100 g), and significant sodium (446 mg/100 g). The glycemic index is approximately 92 (high), making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. The low dietary fibre content (1.2 g/100 g) and high sugar (13.4 g/100 g) further reduce its nutritional suitability for metabolic health conditions. Calcium content is notably high at 211 mg/100 g, reflecting the dairy ingredients. Iron is present at 2.9 mg/100 g from the fortified flour base.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a scone?
What is the GI of a scone?
How much fat is in a buttery scone?
What are the ingredients in a scone?
Is scone good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010283, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat and fatty acid fractions are estimated from the USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy scone entry (USDA #18439) as HPB does not publish fatty acid breakdowns; this introduces a medium-confidence element for those specific fields. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain scone. Overall confidence is rated high given the primary HPB source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (10.5 g/100 g), monounsaturated fat (5.1 g/100 g), polyunsaturated fat (1.2 g/100 g), and trans fat (0.3 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for scones (NDB #18439) as HPB Foodo does not report fatty acid fractions for this item. These values should be verified against a direct laboratory analysis if available. 2. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed vitamin, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this specific HPB Buttery Scone entry are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or confirmed in USDA FoodData Central for an exact match as of 2026-04-16. 3. GI of 92 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for plain scone (white flour, baked); no direct GI measurement for buttery scone was found in the approved GI database. 4. This is a Western-cuisine food; multilingual local-name fields (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not applicable and are set to null. The food is consumed in Singapore as an imported Western item but does not have established local-language names in the Singaporean food lexicon. G6 multilingual compliance is satisfied as this food is classified as Western cuisine, not Singaporean cuisine, and therefore the Singaporean multilingual mandate does not apply.
